story:
Kenny always knew what he wanted: to escape the tired rooms of his childhood, the ache of a father who drifted in and out, a mother who wore herself thin, and the silence left by a brother who was gone too soon. Quiet, nerdy, not very outgoing, he learned to disappear into smoke and music and the pull of the stars, telling himself that if he could just get rich, everything would finally make sense. Now, bass shaking the mirror and his eyes red and low, he cut through space toward Antugua, the planet where his brother Keeyan had died five years ago. The air was wrong the moment he landed. It was the darkest day of the year—twenty-four hours without sunlight as Antugua slipped into its annual shadow—and the yabvacs were screaming in the trees.

He left his PQ-759 and found the path, the marks carved by Keeyan’s old crew. They had lived fast and outside the law—robberies, worse—earning a reputation that earned them enemies. Since Keeyan’s death, the core had crumbled: Z found non-responsive in a small town, Mina and Reena gone too. Kenny had hated their life even before he understood it, but Keeyan had always told him: If anything happens, go to the spot and lay low. So Kenny pushed into the bunker, lit candles, and tore the place apart, hunting for the switch that would reveal the book his brother had promised. He hadn’t wanted to return. He’d moved to Thianus, tried to do honest work, but someone kept watching him. Maybe because of Keeyan. Maybe because the past doesn’t stay buried.

The smoke filled the room as the old conversations replayed. Keeyan smiling, as always, except for the day their father stopped showing up. News on the radio: PenShin—PS—the fugitive, assassinated. Business, Keeyan said. Finish school, walk your own path. I’ll go legit once you’re done. Kenny had argued, begged him to stop. He still remembered the field where Keeyan fell, the surprise trapped in his eyes. I’m sorry I failed, Kenny thought, lighting another match.

“What are you doing here?” a familiar voice asked. Jocelin stepped in from the darkness—short, dark-haired, tough—once close to the crew, the same girl who had helped on Gunabeira before disappearing after the funeral. They fell back into their old rhythm. She’d been taking jobs and laying low. They passed the pipe and took stock: Hark, Qaz, Thea hiding somewhere in the east of the galaxy. That left Jocelin and Gee. Kenny wanted to talk to Gee first. Something had felt off, like Gee knew more about Keeyan’s death than he’d said. Jocelin countered that Gee and Keeyan had made peace. Then her voice broke. The memories here hurt. They’d been a family, against the world. Now they were scattered or gone.

Kenny held her. Their foreheads touched. A spark, a pull. Then they both pulled back, flustered, and went back to work. In a back hall with four emergency tunnels leading to town, they found a bookshelf with a small statue of an old man in a chair, finger raised, pipe in hand. Kenny pushed. Click. The shelf swung open. A book and a note: If you’re reading this, I’m gone, my brother. This will guide you to what I hid for us. Love, Keeyan.

A pistol cocked in the darkness.

“Run!” Jocelin yelled.

They ran. And whoever was out there didn’t stop.
